ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken noodle soup entry; no specific Vietnamese pho ga entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated pho ga code; A0FHR (chicken soup with noodles) is the closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for pho ga identified; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of pho ga is approximately 500 g, comprising roughly 200 g broth, 150 g rice noodles, 100 g chicken slices, and 50 g garnishes (bean sprouts, herbs, lime).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken noodle soup, home-prepared (SR Legacy 06972) and rice noodles (SR Legacy 20065), cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table for pho-style broth dishes
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry for pho ga exists. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy chicken noodle soup (06972) and rice noodle entries, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA table for Southeast Asian broth-based noodle dishes. Input values (energy 80 kcal, protein 7 g, fat 2 g, carbohydrate 8 g, sodium 240 mg) are consistent with this composite and are retained. Micronutrient values (calcium, iron, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat, vitamin C) are also consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for comparable preparations and are retained with medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice noodles (GI ~61) and chicken broth dilution effect; composite GI estimated at 56 for pho-style rice noodle soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for pho ga exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Rice noodles (banh pho) have a published GI of approximately 61 (medium) per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The composite dish GI is estimated at 56 (medium) accounting for the dilution effect of the protein-rich broth (7 g protein/100 g), lean chicken, and low-sugar garnishes, which moderate the overall glycaemic response relative to plain rice noodles. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (56 × 40 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 22.4 (high GL for the full bowl, consistent with a noodle-dominant meal).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 240 mg/100 g; a 500 g bowl delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium, representing 52–60% of the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, consume no more than half the broth, and avoid adding table condiments such as hoisin or chilli sauce to keep the per-meal sodium load below 600 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles (banh pho) have a medium-to-high GI (~61); a standard 500 g bowl provides approximately 40 g available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~22, which is high for a single meal
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should request a smaller noodle portion (half serve) with extra broth and bean sprouts to reduce the glycemic load, avoid adding hoisin or sweet chilli sauce, and pair the meal with a protein-forward side to blunt the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is very low at 0.6 g/100 g, trans fat is negligible at 0.02 g/100 g, and the dish is broth-based with lean chicken protein; the primary cardiovascular concern is sodium load from the broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pho ga is a heart-friendly choice due to its low saturated fat and lean protein profile; patients with CVD should limit broth consumption to half the bowl to manage sodium intake and avoid atherogenic condiment additions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (ginger, star anise, cinnamon, coriander) used in pho broth; nightshade garnishes (chilli) may trigger flares in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pho ga is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile and very low saturated fat; patients should omit chilli garnish if nightshade sensitivity is suspected and request reduced fish sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pho Ga (Phở Gà) is a traditional Vietnamese chicken noodle soup consisting of a clear, aromatic broth simmered with whole chicken, charred ginger, charred onion, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and coriander seeds. The broth is ladled over flat rice noodles (bánh phở) and topped with thinly sliced poached chicken breast or thigh meat. The dish is served with a garnish plate of fresh bean sprouts, Thai basil, sawtooth coriander, lime wedges, and sliced chilli. Fish sauce and sometimes a small amount of rock sugar are used to season the broth. Per 100 g, pho ga provides approximately 80 kcal, 7 g protein, 2 g fat (0.6 g saturated), and 8 g carbohydrate, with 240 mg sodium. It is naturally gluten-free (rice noodles) and dairy-free. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~56) driven by the rice noodles, but the high protein content from lean chicken moderates the glycaemic response. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium from fish sauce and broth; a full 500 g bowl delivers approximately 1200 mg sodium. The anti-inflammatory spice blend (ginger, star anise, cinnamon) provides phytochemical benefit relevant to inflammatory conditions. Pho ga is widely consumed in Vietnam and across Southeast Asia, and is a popular menu item in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore and globally.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry for pho ga exists. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy chicken noodle soup and rice noodle entries, cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. Input values are consistent with these references and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from published rice noodle GI data. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for pho ga are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or FAO/INFOODS SEA table as of 2026. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported for this composite dish preparation in the approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA). potassium_mg value of 220 mg/100 g is an estimate from USDA SR Legacy chicken noodle soup and is consistent with the input record's stated estimate; retained with medium confidence. trans_fat_g of 0.02 g/100 g is a trace value from the input record, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for similar preparations; retained. The input record listed two duplicate portion entries both labelled '1 bowl' with different weights (600 g and 500 g); the 500 g value is used as the standard serving size as it is more consistent with typical Vietnamese restaurant pho ga portions. The input record's country_of_origin was listed as Singapore; this has been corrected to Vietnam as the dish's culinary origin, while noting it is widely served in Singapore. Gluten-free flag is set true as traditional pho ga uses rice noodles; however, cross-contamination risk exists in restaurant settings where wheat-based noodles are also prepared.
